_comment: One file per procedure_code. Distilled from the matching pair in PM JAY/Standard Treatment Guideline/master/<condition>/ : _Medco.pdf (clinical + mandatory docs) + _PPD.CPD.pdf (PPD/CPD processing checklists). Filename = procedure_code. This is what the Documentation Checker, Query Predictor, and Medical Justification agents read AFTER the picker has chosen the code.
procedure_name: Surgical Correction of Total Anomalous Pulmonary Venous Connection (TAPVC) Repair
clinical_indications: Patients with obstructive TAPVC should undergo emergency surgery.
Surgery should be performed as early as possible in nonobstructive TAPVC, even if they are asymptomatic.
Those presenting late should be evaluated for onset of pulmonary vascular disease and operated if the data suggest operable status.

ppd_preauth.q: Clinical notes contain detailed history, signs & symptoms, and indication for procedure?
Echo/Doppler report is suggestive of TAPVC?

cpd_claim.q: Post procedure ECHO shows repair of the defect?
Detailed Discharge Summary with follow-up advice available?

common_queries: Echo/Doppler report not suggestive of TAPVC at pre-authorization stage
Clinical notes missing or lacking detailed history, signs & symptoms, or indication for procedure
Post-procedure ECHO report does not show repair of the defect at claim stage
Detailed Discharge Summary missing or lacking follow-up advice at claim stage

clinical_key_pointers: Total Anomalous Pulmonary Venous Connection (TAPVC) occurs in 0.5–0.9/10,000 live births and accounts for 1% of all patients born with congenital heart defects.
An ASD is necessary for survival.
TAPVC is classified into four types depending on the site of drainage: Supracardiac (45%-50%), cardiac (15%-20%), infracardiac (26%-28%), and mixed (5%-8%).
Obstruction to the drainage of pulmonary veins is most common in the infracardiac variety and least common in the cardiac type.
If not treated, TAPVC has a very high mortality with 85% dying in the 1st year of life. Median survival was 2 months (range 1 day to 49 years).
Obstructive TAPVC significantly reduces median survival from 2.5 months (nonobstructive) to 3 weeks (obstructive).
Obstructive TAPVC is the only cyanotic CHD where prostaglandin infusion should be avoided.

Clinical assessment: Neonates with severe obstruction present with cyanosis and respiratory distress soon after birth (usually within 12 h). Patients with no obstruction present with clinical features of large left-to-right shunts with mild cyanosis.
Pulse oximetry is used for quantifying cyanosis, which may give an estimate of PVR.
ECG typically shows right-axis deviation with right ventricular hypertrophy.

X-ray chest shows cardiomegaly, prominent pulmonary artery segment, and pulmonary plethora. Older infants and children with supracardiac TAPVC may show a 'figure of 8' or 'snowman sign'. Neonates with obstructive TAPVC show no or minimal cardiomegaly and severe pulmonary venous hypertension (ground glass appearance).

Echocardiography is the investigation of choice, providing complete information in most patients. It shows dilated right atrium, RV, and pulmonary artery. Key finding is inability to show connection of pulmonary veins to left atrium and right-to-left shunting through an ASD. Defines site of drainage and obstruction. Assesses pulmonary artery pressure and identifies associated anomalies.

Cardiac catheterization is rarely performed, but may be required in patients with pulmonary artery hypertension presenting beyond infancy where operability is in doubt. An arterial saturation of >85% and a PVR (indexed) of <8 WU.m² may indicate that the patient is operable.
CTA/cMRI are reserved for patients where echocardiography is inconclusive, such as in mixed type of TAPVC.

Important determinants of long-term prognosis include residual pulmonary vein stenosis, residual pulmonary hypertension, progressive stenosis of surgically created anastomosis, and late-onset arrhythmias. Pulmonary venous obstruction occurs in 5%-15% of patients after surgical repair.
